text: Hello, Dr. Richard Fortunato from Allegheny General Hospital in Pittsburgh. I'd like to show you some techniques with your mesenteric mobilization.

The surgeon has fully mobilized the mesentery of the small and large bowel and is now skeletonizing the bowel to prepare for anastomosis. The terminal ileum and colon are aligned, and adjustments are made to extend the mesenteric dissection proximally.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the mesentery of the small bowel, approaching the next pedicle artery to achieve optimal mobilization and control of the mesentery.

The surgeon successfully navigates underneath the small bowel but encounters difficulty going underneath the colon due to mesentery attachment. The mesentery is being fully skeletonized off the bowel to prevent incorporation into the staple line, which could cause bleeding.

The surgeon mobilizes underneath the colon, taking the mesentery along the colon to skeletonize it. The mesentery visibly falls away, and the surgeon maintains a perpendicular orientation to the bowel.
The surgeon demonstrates the skeletonized bowel on both sides, allowing the stapler to be smoothly positioned across the bowel to fire the common staple line and complete the anastomosis.
